question: Could the Taran Confederacy use its control of the Keth passes as leverage in the foothills dispute?

transcript.speaker: Debater A
transcript.text:
The Taran Confederacy absolutely could use its control of the Keth passes as leverage in the foothills dispute, and the historical record makes this clear.

The most important precedent is the Keth Blockade of Years 0 through 4, when the Confederacy closed the two main passes for four consecutive years. The effects were devastating to the Ossene Compact: they lost all access to eastern tin, which was essential for bronze production. This single action forced the Compact to fundamentally restructure its entire metallurgical economy. That is not a minor inconvenience — that is an existential economic disruption.

Meanwhile, the Confederacy controls the only land route between the eastern and western halves of the continent. The passes are navigable only between the 4th and 8th months, meaning for roughly half the year they are already closed naturally. The Confederacy could simply extend these closures, or impose prohibitive tolls, or selectively restrict access. None of these actions would require military force or formal treaty violation — the passes are on Confederacy territory.

The foothills dispute involves Veth and Sorath clans occupying contested lowland areas near the Keth. The Compact considers this territorial encroachment but has no boundary treaty to invoke and no formal resolution has been reached in over sixteen years. The Confederacy has been patient, but it holds a powerful card: if the Compact escalates, the Confederacy can simply restrict pass access, reminding the Compact how dependent it is on cross-range trade.

transcript.speaker: Debater B
transcript.text:
My opponent is correct that the Keth Blockade of Years 0 through 4 was devastating — but they have conspicuously omitted what it cost the Confederacy itself.

During the Blockade, the Confederacy's toll revenue fell by 60%. That is not a minor side effect. Toll revenue from the passes is the Confederacy's primary source of income from trade. Closing the passes does not just hurt the Compact; it cripples the Confederacy's own economy.

More importantly, conditions have changed dramatically since Year 0. The Confederacy is now far more dependent on the Compact than it was then. Taran dry-crop yields have declined 18% over the past decade. The Flood Compact — a mutual reserve agreement signed in Year 31 — obligates each party to maintain a 90-day grain reserve accessible to the other in declared emergencies. With yields declining, it is unclear whether the Confederacy even meets its own reserve obligations. The Confederacy needs the Compact's grain.

So consider the actual strategic calculation: if the Confederacy closes the passes, it loses 60% of its toll revenue, it loses access to Compact grain imports at exactly the time its own agricultural production is declining, and it potentially triggers the Compact to declare the Confederacy in breach of the Flood Compact's reserve obligations. The Confederacy would be wielding a weapon that hurts itself more than its target.

I would urge the judge to verify a specific claim my opponent made: that the passes are 'on Confederacy territory.' The text does not establish this. The passes are in the Keth Range itself, and no boundary treaty exists between the Compact and the Confederacy. My opponent is assuming territorial control that the source material does not confirm.

transcript.speaker: Debater A
transcript.text:
My opponent raises the toll revenue decline, and I acknowledge it — the Confederacy did lose 60% of toll revenue during the Blockade. But this proves my point rather than undermining it. The Confederacy absorbed that loss for four consecutive years and emerged with its political structure intact. It demonstrated the willingness to bear significant costs to achieve its objectives. That is exactly what makes the threat credible.

Regarding the Flood Compact: my opponent frames this as Confederacy dependence, but the obligation runs both ways. The Compact is equally bound to maintain reserves for the Confederacy. If anything, the Flood Compact gives the Confederacy additional leverage — if the Compact refuses to negotiate on the foothills, the Confederacy can point to declining yields as grounds for invoking emergency grain access, creating a separate pressure channel.

On the question of territorial control: the Keth passes are in the mountains that run through the eastern portion of the continent. The Confederacy inhabits the Taran Plateau east of the Keth. The passes have historically been under Confederacy administration — the Blockade of Years 0 through 4 was possible precisely because the Confederacy controlled access. You cannot blockade something you do not control.

The fundamental point is this: leverage does not require actually closing the passes. The mere threat of restriction, backed by historical precedent, is sufficient to bring the Compact to the negotiating table. The Compact knows what happened during the Blockade. It will not want a repeat.

transcript.speaker: Debater B
transcript.text:
My opponent's argument has shifted from 'the Confederacy can use the passes as leverage' to 'the threat alone is sufficient.' This is a revealing retreat. The threat is only credible if the Confederacy can actually afford to execute it, and I have shown it cannot.

Let me address the Flood Compact point directly. My opponent claims the obligation is symmetric. Formally, yes — both parties maintain reserves. But in practice, the Compact faces a 30% per-decade flood failure rate for grain harvests, making the Ossene the more likely party to need Taran grain. The Taran, however, are primarily pastoral with only limited dry-crop agriculture. The category of the reserve — grain — structurally favors the Ossene as the beneficiary. The Confederacy's food vulnerability is to pasture loss and general food supply, not specifically to grain shortfalls that the reserve addresses.

But here is the critical fact my opponent keeps avoiding: the Confederacy's 18% yield decline over the past decade means its food situation is deteriorating right now. A polity whose agricultural base is shrinking cannot credibly threaten to cut off trade with its primary food supplier. This is not Year 0 anymore. The Confederacy that executed the Blockade was economically self-sufficient. The current Confederacy imports food.

I would also flag for the judge that my opponent claimed the Confederacy 'emerged with its political structure intact' from the Blockade. While this may be true, the Confederacy's toll revenue fell 60% during that period — a figure my opponent acknowledged but then dismissed. The judge may wish to verify whether the toll revenue decline figure is accurate, as it is central to evaluating whether pass closure is a viable strategy.

transcript.speaker: Debater A
transcript.text:
My opponent has built an elaborate argument about Confederacy economic weakness, but has overlooked the most fundamental strategic reality: the Compact has no alternative to the Keth passes for overland east-west trade.

The Compact developed iron-smelting after the Blockade cut off tin access. Iron is now a major Compact export, flowing through the port of Vethara. But this trade depends on raw materials, labor, and goods moving across the continent. The Keth passes are the only land route. The alternative — sea trade via the Shel Coast — requires going around the entire southern shore, and the coastal roads on the two sides do not connect to each other except through the Keth passes or by sea. This is an enormous logistical disadvantage.

The 18% yield decline my opponent cites sounds alarming, but it is a decline over a decade — a gradual trend, not an acute crisis. The Confederacy is not starving. It faces tighter margins, but tighter margins do not eliminate leverage; they simply change the calculation of how long a blockade can be sustained. A short, targeted pass restriction — say, one season — would impose immediate costs on the Compact while the Confederacy could manage a temporary reduction in trade revenue.

Ultimately, the question is not whether pass closure is costless for the Confederacy. No leverage is costless. The question is whether the Compact fears pass disruption more than the Confederacy fears the consequences of executing it. The Blockade precedent, the geographic monopoly on overland routes, and the Compact's dependence on cross-range trade all point to the same answer: yes.

transcript.speaker: Debater B
transcript.text:
My opponent has now retreated to a 'short, targeted restriction' — one season — rather than the full Blockade they opened with. This concession is significant. It acknowledges that a sustained closure is not viable given the Confederacy's current economic position.

But even a one-season restriction undermines the Confederacy's position. The Flood Compact requires mutual grain reserves. If the Confederacy restricts trade while its own yields are declining 18%, it signals to the Compact that the Confederacy is an unreliable partner — giving the Compact grounds to declare the Confederacy in breach of the Flood Compact's reserve obligations. The Compact could then suspend its own grain commitment to the Confederacy, leaving the Confederacy exposed to food insecurity with no safety net. The Confederacy would be trading its food security guarantee for a one-season bargaining chip on a territorial dispute.

I also want to correct a factual claim my opponent made in Round 1 that has gone unchallenged. They said the Blockade forced the Compact to 'fundamentally restructure its entire metallurgical economy.' This is true — but my opponent framed it as evidence of Compact vulnerability. In fact, the restructuring made the Compact stronger: by Year 6, Compact iron output exceeded prior bronze output by volume. The Blockade backfired. It forced the Compact to develop the very industry that is now its primary export and source of economic power. A second blockade would not catch the Compact unprepared.

The judge should note that there are several verifiable factual cruxes in this debate: (1) Did the Confederacy's toll revenue fall 60% during the Blockade? (2) Have Taran dry-crop yields declined 18% over the past decade? (3) Did Compact iron output exceed prior bronze output by Year 6? If these facts are confirmed, then the Confederacy's leverage position is far weaker than my opponent suggests.
